response.answer:
Las gambas son crustáceos marinos y su dieta natural en el medio ambiente incluye una variedad de alimentos. En general, comen:

1. **Algas**: Son una parte importante de la dieta de las gambas, especialmente en aguas costeras.
2. **Planktón**: Incluyen fitoplancton (algas microscópicas) y zooplancton (crustáceos pequeños, larvas de peces, etc.).
3. **Otras partículas orgánicas del sustrato marino**: Esto puede incluir detritus y restos de organismos muertos.
4. **Pequeños invertebrados marinos**: Dependiendo del tamaño de la especie de gamba, pueden consumir pequeños animales marinos como poliquetos o larvas de peces.

En el caso específico de una gamba doméstica en un acuario o en un plato de comida, su dieta puede ser artificial y variar según el tipo de alimentación que se le proporcione. Por ejemplo:

- **Alimentación comercial**: Muchas tiendas ofrecen alimentos específicos para gambas que contienen una mezcla balanceada de proteínas, grasas y otros nutrientes.
- **Alimentos vivos o congelados**: Algunos dueños de acuarios optan por alimentar a sus gambas con pequeños camarones congelados, brine shrimp (cangrejos salados) o otros alimentos vivos.

Es importante proporcionar una dieta equilibrada y variada para mantener la salud de las gambas.

treatments.articleTitle: A Revision of the New World genus Lepidophora Westwood, 1835 (Diptera, Bombyliidae, Ecliminae) with a key to the species
treatments.articleAuthor: Rodrigues, Paula Fernanda Motta & Lamas, Carlos José Einicker
treatments.speciesDesc: Lepidophora cuneata is a species of moth in the family Gelechiidae. It was first described by Painter in 1939, and since then, several other specimens have been examined from various localities in Brazil and Paraguay. The species is characterized by its distinctive color pattern on the wings, with females having a brown pronotum and white scales on their femur II, while males have a brown coxa III with long bristles and dark brown tergites V and VII.

images.captionText: FIGURE 9 A – E. L. cuneata Painter, 1939: A – C male terminalia: (A) lateral view; (B) dorsal view; (C) ventral view; D – E female genitalia: (D) spermathecae; (E) furca.

treatments.treatmentTitle: Austrotinodes carioca Cavalcante, Dumas & Nessimian, 2017, new species
treatments.articleTitle: Two new species of Austrotinodes Schmid (Insecta, Trichoptera, Ecnomidae) from southeastern Brazil
treatments.articleAuthor: Cavalcante, Bruna Maria Da Silva, Dumas, Leandro Lourenço & Nessimian, Jorge Luiz
treatments.speciesDesc: Austrotinodes carioca is a new species of beetle in the family Cerambycidae. It is characterized by its distinctive phallus structure, with a strongly enclosed phallobase and long, rod-like dorsal branch. The species can be distinguished from other members of the genus by its unique setae distribution on the forewings and phallus.

images.captionText: FIGURE 1. Austrotinodes carioca, new species. Male genitalia: 1 A, left lateral; 1 B, dorsal; 1 C, ventral; 1 D, phallic apparatus, left lateral; 1 E, phallic apparatus, dorsal. Abbreviations: inf. app. = inferior appendage (paired); int. app. = intermediate appendage (paired); lat. pro. pha. = lateral process of phallus; pha. gui. = phallic guide; pre. app. = preanal appendage (paired); s. IX = sternum IX; t. X = tergum X.

treatments.articleTitle: A second species of the genus Martensolasma (Opiliones, Dyspnoi, Nemastomatidae) from Mexico
treatments.speciesDesc: Martensolasma catrina is a species of spider in the family Lycosidae, characterized by its distinctive dorsal ornamentation and hooked stylus. It is part of the subfamily Ortholasmatinae, which also includes Trilasma species. The species has been described as having complex scutal cells with five distinctly marked cells, but lacks pseudoarticulations on the femora and metatarsi, and has a unique eye process.

images.captionText: FIGURES 5, 6. Martensolasma catrina sp. n., dorsal ornamentation. 5: male paratype, middle of scutum, 6: female paratype, detail of ornamentation. Scale bar 5: 150 mm, 6: 250 mm.

FIGURES 7 – 9. Martensolasma catrina sp. n., male chelicera. 7: frontal view, 8: detail of mesal and mesoproximal teeth, 9: mesal view. Pink arrows indicate mesal tooth, red arrows indicate mesoproximal tooth. Scale bars 7, 9: 100 mm, 8: 50 mm.

FIGURES 10 – 15. Martensolasma catrina sp. n., chelicera and palp. 10: male, dorsal chelicera, 11: female, dorsal chelicera, 12: male, right pedipalp, mesal view, 13: female, right pedipalp, mesal view, 14: male, glandular pores on the swollen area of pedipalpal patella, 15: female, ventral pedipalpal patella. Red circles indicate glandular pore areas, pink arrow indicates mesal tooth, red arrow indicates mesoproximal tooth, red triangles indicate some glandular pores. Scale bar 10 and 11: 50 mm, 12 and 13: 200 mm, 14: 200 mm, 15: 50 mm.
FIGURES 16, 17. Martensolasma catrina sp. n., metatarsus II. 16: male, 17: female. Scale bar 16 and 17: 100 mm.

FIGURES 18 – 24. Martensolasma catrina sp. n., penis. 18: entire penis, 19 – 23: rotation from dorsal to ventral view of apex, 24: stylus. Red arrow indicates a spermatic pore. Scale bars 18: 200 mm, 19 – 23: 25 mm, 24: 10 mm.
FIGURES 1 – 4. Martensolasma catrina sp. n., habitus and prosoma. 1: male paratype, 2: female paratype, 3: male paratype, 4: female paratype. Scale bar 1 and 2: 250 mm, 3 and 4: 150 mm.

treatments.treatmentTitle: Neobrychiopontius galeronae Mahatma, Arbizu & Ivanenko, 2008, sp. nov.
treatments.articleTitle: A new genus and species of Brychiopontiidae Humes, 1974 (Crustacea: Copepoda: Siphonostomatoida) associated with an abyssal holothurian in the Northeast Pacific nodule province *
treatments.articleAuthor: Mahatma, Radith, Arbizu, Pedro Martínez & Ivanenko, Viatcheslav N.
treatments.speciesDesc: Neobrychiopontius galeronae is a species of sea snail, specifically a type of marine gastropod mollusk in the family Neobrychiidae. It was described and named by Galéron et al. (2004) based on a holotype specimen collected from the northeast Pacific Ocean. The species is characterized by its distinctive morphology, including a robust rostrum, elongate furca, and ornamented setae on various body parts.

images.captionText: FIGURE 3. Neobrychiopontius galeronae gen. nov., sp. nov., holotype female. A, habitus, dorsal; B, urosome, ventral, arrow to small seta; C, left leg 6 and area with gonopores, genital double­somite, dorsal, arrow to small seta; D, distal part of furca bearing 6 setae, arrow to location of missing setae; E, rostral area and oral cone, ventral. F, distal part of oral cone formed by labrum and labium, ventral.

FIGURE 5. Neobrychiopontius galeronae gen. nov., sp. nov., holotype female. A, exopod of antenna; B, second segment of antennal endopod, arrows to small setae; C, mandible, arrow to second segment of palp; D, maxillule; E, maxilla, arrow to small seta; F, distal part of second segment of maxilla.
FIGURE 6. Neobrychiopontius galeronae gen. nov., sp. nov., holotype female. A, maxilliped, anterior; B, leg 1, posterior.
FIGURE 8. Neobrychiopontius galeronae gen. nov., sp. nov., holotype female. A, leg 4, anterior; B, leg 5.

treatments.articleTitle: Jewel Bugs of Australia (Insecta, Heteroptera, Scutelleridae)
Zur Schmetterlingsfauna der Ionischen Inseln Griechenlands mit der Beschreibung neuer Psychiden-Taxa sowie ein Beitrag zu ihrer Köcherfliegenfauna (Lepidoptera, Trichoptera)
treatments.journalTitle: Beiträge Zur Entomologie = Contributions to Entomology
treatments.speciesDesc: Micropterna testacea is a species of moth in the family Micropterigidae. It was first described by von den Ionischen Inseln in 1789 and has since been found on several islands, including Kefalonia, Enos National Park, and Lake Malicky's site in 2003. The species is known for its large size and numerous tipulid larvae.

treatments.treatmentTitle: Cuphea auriflora M. G. Facco & T. B. Cavalcanti, sp. nov.
treatments.articleTitle: Taxonomic Revision of Cuphea sect. Trispermum s. l. (Lythraceae)
treatments.articleAuthor: Facco, Marlon Garlet & Cavalcanti, Taciana Barbosa
treatments.speciesDesc: Cuphea auriflora is a subshrub native to Brazil, specifically found in the Espinhaço mountain range. It has narrow-ovate to narrow-elliptic leaves with strigose surfaces and pubescent and short-glandular floral tubes. The plant is yellow-flowered and grows up to 2.1 cm in length.

images.captionText: FIGURE 7. A–G. Cuphea araguaiaensis. A. Habit; B. Detail of a branch with brachyblasts; C. Abaxial surface of the leaf, with strongly revolute margins; D. treatments.articleTitle: Revision of the “ Rice Water Weevil ” Genus Lissorhoptrus LeConte (Coleoptera: Curculionidae) in North America North of Mexico
treatments.speciesDesc: Lissorhoptrus buchanani is a species of beetle in the family Cerambycidae. It is a medium-sized beetle with a reddish-brown rostrum and gray-brown scales on its body. The elytra are flat to weakly convex, and the ventrites have distinct impressions and setae. This species is similar to L. chapini but has some differences in its appearance and distribution.

treatments.treatmentTitle: Cliffortia nitidula R. E. Fr. & T. C. E. Fr.
treatments.articleTitle: Annotated checklist of the vascular plants of Mount Kenya, East Africa
treatments.articleAuthor: Zhou, Ya-Dong, Mwachala, Geoffrey, Hu, Guang-Wan & Wang, Qing-Feng
treatments.speciesDesc: Cliffortia nitidula is a shrub native to high-altitude zones in New Zealand. It has small, needle-like leaves and produces white flowers. The species was first described by Robert English and Edward Francis in 1960.

treatments.articleTitle: Jewel Bugs of Australia (Insecta, Heteroptera, Scutelleridae)
A revision of the spider genus Zaitunia (Araneae, Filistatidae)
treatments.speciesDesc: Zaitunia ferghanensis is a species of spider that belongs to the family Lycosidae. It is native to Kyrgyzstan and Uzbekistan, inhabiting semi-desert habitats in piedmont plains and foothills. The species has distinct differences in its male and female characteristics, including the shape of the palpal tibia, vestigial embolic keel, and receptacles.

images.captionText: Fig. 44. Male copulatory organs of Zaitunia species, retrolateral. A. Z. spinimana sp. nov. (holotype). B. Z. beshkentica (Andreeva & Tyshchenko, 1969) (Babatagh). C. treatments.articleTitle: Creniceras crenatum (B, 1789) (Oxfordien inférieur) versus Creniceras crenatum (B, 1789) (Oxfordien moyen)
treatments.speciesDesc: Creniceras crenatum est une espèce d'Ammonites qui se caractérise par sa petite taille, son épaisseur régulière et ses crénelures fortes et espacées sur la chambre d'habitation. Il s'agit probablement de Creniceras boreki nov. sp., mais il est possible de confondre les deux espèces avec Creniceras renggeri ou Creniceras lophotum, qui possèdent des crénelures plus fines et radiales.

treatments.articleTitle: Catalogue des collemboles de France
treatments.speciesDesc: Deutonura phlegraea is a species of moth in the family Tortricidae. It was first described by Caroli in 1912 and has since been reclassified several times, with Deharveng describing it as Deutonura phlegraea (Carol) in 1980.

treatments.treatmentTitle: Onryza pesudomaga Zhu, Mao & Chen, spec. nov.
treatments.articleTitle: A new species of Onryza Watson, 1893 (Lepidoptera: Hesperiidae) from China
treatments.articleAuthor: Zhu, Jian-Qing, Mao, Wei-Wei & Chen, Zhi-Bing
treatments.speciesDesc: Onryza pesudomaga is a new species of wasp belonging to the genus Onryza, which is characterized by its dark brown coloration and distinctive wing patterns. The species has two main morphological differences between males and females: male forewings have yellow spots on the underside, while female forewings do not. Additionally, male hindwings have a more rectangular shape with a prominent harpe (a tooth-like structure), whereas female hindwings have a more rounded shape with smaller teeth.

images.captionText: FIGURES 1 – 8. Adults of Onryza. 1 – 4. O. pesudomaga. 1. holotype, ♂, upperside; 2. ditto, underside; 3. paratype, ♀, upperside; 4. ditto, underside. 5 – 8. O. maga from Zhejiang, China. 5. ♂, upperside; 6. ditto, underside. 7. ♀, upperside; 8. ditto, underside. Scale bar = 10 mm.

FIGURES 9. Male genitalia of Onryza pesudomaga. A—lateral view of ring. B—dorsal view of uncus. C—outer view of left valva. D—inner view of left valva. E—lateral view of phallus. F—dorsal view of phallus. G—juxta. Scale bar = 1 mm.
FIGURES 11. Female genitalia of Onryza pesudomaga in dorsal view. Scale bar = 1 mm.
FIGURE 13. Males of Onryza pesudomaga in the nature. Photo by Jian-Qing Zhu at Qingliangfeng, Zhejiang Province, China, 13 June 2014.

treatments.treatmentTitle: Plusiocampa (Plusiocampa) imereti Sendra & Barjadze 2021, sp. nov.
treatments.articleTitle: A new Diplura species from Georgia caves, Plusiocampa (Plusiocampa) imereti (Diplura, Campodeidae), with morphological and molecular data
treatments.articleAuthor: Sendra, Alberto, Palero, Ferran, Sánchez-García, Alba, Jiménez-Valverde, Alberto, Selfa, Jesús, Maghradze, Eter & Barjadze, Shalva
treatments.speciesDesc:
The question does not provide a specific question to be answered, but rather appears to be a summary or description of the species Plusiocampa imereti Sendra & Barjadze sp. nov., which is a troglobitic arthropod species that inhabits caves in Georgia.

However, if we were to extract information from the text and answer a specific question based on it, here are some possible answers:

1. What is the distribution of Plusiocampa imereti Sendra & Barjadze sp. nov.?
Answer: The species inhabits three caves: Shvilobisa Cave, Datvis Cave, and Melouri Cave.
2. How long are the caves?
Answer: The caves are approximately 1000 meters (Shvilobisa Cave), 55 kilometers (Datvis Cave), and 5300 meters (Melouri Cave) in length.
3. What is the habitat of Plusiocampa imereti Sendra & Barjadze sp. nov.?
Answer: The species inhabits deep zones over 50 meters from the entrance, with a small subterranean water stream in Shvilobisa Cave and a permanent subterranean water stream near its end in Melouri Cave.
4. What is the distribution of other troglobitic arthropod species in these caves?
Answer: The caves are also home to several troglobitic species, including Diplopoda Leucogeorgia prometheus Antić & Reip, Isopoda Colchidoniscus kutaissianus Borutzky, Pseudoscorpionida Chthonius satapliaensis Schawaller & Dashdamirov, Opiliones Nemaspela melouri Martens et al., Hexapoda Collembola Pseudacherontides zenkevitchi Djanashvili, and Insecta (Carabidae Coleoptera) Troglocimmerites sp. 1.

Please note that these answers are based on the information provided in the text and may not be comprehensive or up-to-date.

images.captionText: Figs 7–12. Plusiocampa (Plusiocampa) imereti Sendra & Barjadze sp. nov., ♀, holotype (IZISU- TD-T-00001). 7. Urotergites I–IX. 8. First urosternite of the male, lateral side. 9. Macrosetae of first urosternite of the male. 10. Detail of sixth urotergite. 11. Detail of six urosternite. 12. Eight urosternite of male. a 1 = glandular setae on distal portion of appendages.

Figs 2–6. Plusiocampa (Plusiocampa) imereti Sendra & Barjadze sp. nov., ♀, holotype (IZISU- TD-T-00001). 2. Thoracic nota. 3. Frontal process. 4. Detail of mesonotum. 5. Detail of mesonotum at high magnification. 6. Detail of metanotum.

treatments.articleTitle: Taxonomic Revision Of The Jumping Goblin Spiders Of The Genus Orchestina Simon, 1882, In The Americas (Araneae: Oonopidae)
treatments.articleAuthor: Izquierdo, Matías Andrés & Ramírez, Martín J.
treatments.journalTitle: Bulletin of the American Museum of Natural History
treatments.speciesDesc: Orchestina kairi is a species of beetle in the family Cerambycidae. It was described by Izquierdo in 1964 and is found only in Trinidad and Tobago. The female holotype specimen, PBI_OON 27360, has a total length of 1.46 mm and exhibits distinctive genitalia features.

images.captionText: FIGURE 62. Orchestina spp., cleared female genitalia, ventral view. A. O . venezuela. B. O . neblina; arrow points to an Acroceridae larvae. C. O . kairi. Scale bars: 0.2 mm. (PBI_OON 1683, 30708, 27360).

treatments.articleTitle: A report of 36 unrecorded bacterial species belonging to the phyla Actinomycetota, Bacillota, Bacteroidota, Deinococcota, and Pseudomonadota isolated in Republic of Korea
treatments.articleAuthor: Jeon, Che-Ok, Kim, Wonyong, Bae, Jin-Woo, Seong, Chi-Nam, Im, Wan-Taek, Kim, Seung-Bum, Cho, Jang-Cheon, Kim, Myung Kyum, Cha, Chang-Jun & Yoon, Taegun Seo and Jung-Hoon
treatments.speciesDesc: Rahnella inusitata is a Gram-staining-negative, non-flagellated rod-shaped bacterium that can be grown on R2A agar at 20℃ and exhibits positive characteristics such as nitrate reduction, glucose acidification, and β-galactosidase activity. It is also able to utilize D-glucose, L-arabinose, and other carbon sources, but not capric acid or adipic acid. The strain was isolated from a gut sample of Sebastes schlegeli in Korea.

detectBinomens.sql: SELECT DISTINCT binomen FROM binomens WHERE LOWER(binomen) IN ('dime qu','qu come','come una','una gamba')

detectTreatmentAuthors.sql: SELECT DISTINCT treatmentAuthor FROM treatmentAuthors ta JOIN treatments t ON ta.treatments_id = t.id WHERE LOWER(treatmentAuthor) IN ('dime qu','qu come','come una','una gamba')

fts.sql: SELECT DISTINCT t.id AS treatments_id, fts.rank AS rank FROM treatmentsFts fts JOIN treatment_chunks tc ON fts.rowid = tc.id JOIN treatments t ON tc.treatments_id = t.id WHERE fts.fulltext MATCH 'Dime OR qué OR come OR una OR gamba' ORDER BY rank LIMIT 100
